flavor_text: Frayed cord and a pocket of bad leather throw stones badly. Hunger makes you keep trying.
Dry air makes the cast crack. The sound keeps time when nerves do not.
Briar knots bite the palm. Patience costs less than bandages.
A small bow forgives bad form. It feeds the first weeks of hunger.
Fog soaked limbs stay quiet, the string feels like wet rope. It still holds.
Mud stained wraps and stubborn draw survive ditches better than men.
Tally cuts from long watches keep the hand honest. Silence does the rest.
Thornwood nocks bite the finger. Slow down, or bleed.
Hard pull and fast return make distance feel less cruel for a moment.
A chipped bead whistles on the spin, just enough to steady breath.
Dark stain vanishes against bark, only the arrow gives you away.
Built for running shots, the grip is polished by panic and rain.
Made for open sky, it rewards calm hands and punishes haste.
Soft pouch and dead cord keep quiet. A loud mistake would be final.
Charcoal smears on the grip tell old fires. Wind turns mean, the draw stays true.
Steel tucked into wood and sinew keeps its shape through cold mornings.
Splinters under the wrap warn restless fingers. Fidgeting pays in blood.
Scarred at the handle from a bad day, it still draws without complaint.
Braided by sleepless hands, the sling feels ready at any hour. You learn to match it.
A pale burn on one limb tells its history. Work continues without apology.
Smoke in the grain and a steady center turn doubt into a straight line.
Heavy and steady, it was made for the last arrow you can afford. Take the shot.
Wrapped dark to hide the hands, it belongs to someone who learned to move unseen.
Straight in the draw and cruel in the release, distance starts feeling like power.
Built for one clean kill at dusk. Silence is the price, certainty is the return.
